title: The Zabbaleen of Cairo
summary: An informal community in Cairo recycles more household waste than the modern municipal contractor. The official solution missed something the unofficial one knew.
body:
In a neighbourhood on the south-eastern edge of Cairo called Manshiyat Naser, about seventy thousand people live and work as the Zabbaleen. The word means, in Egyptian Arabic, 'the garbage people'. For more than a century, families in this community have collected household waste from across the city. They sort it by hand at home. They keep pigs, which eat the organic part. They sell the paper, glass, plastic and metal to small workshops, many of them on the same streets where the sorting happens. By independent estimates, they recycle about eighty per cent of what they collect.

In 2003, the city signed contracts with three large international waste companies. The contracts were modern; the equipment was modern; the trucks were new. The companies promised, in the language of municipal procurement, a 'clean, efficient solution'. By the contract terms, the companies were obliged to recycle twenty per cent of what they collected.

The contracts did not, at first, include the Zabbaleen. The companies were given exclusive zones. In those zones, by law, only the contracted company could collect waste. The Zabbaleen, working in those same streets for generations, found themselves trespassing on routes they had walked all their lives.

The results, after five years, were measurable. The international companies were, on average, recycling between eleven and nineteen per cent of what they collected. The Zabbaleen, working informally in the zones they could still reach, were still recycling around eighty per cent. Cairo, in total, recycled less waste than it had before the modern contracts arrived.

The reason is not mysterious. The international companies were measured on collection — on whether the bins were emptied on time. They were paid the same whether they recycled or not. Recycling was a cost; landfill was cheaper. The Zabbaleen, by contrast, did not get paid for collection. They got paid for what they recovered. Every kilogram of plastic was their income. Their incentive ran in the direction of recycling because their wages did.

In 2009 a separate crisis — a panic about swine flu — led the Egyptian government to order the slaughter of the Zabbaleen's pigs. The pigs had been eating Cairo's organic waste for a century. Within months, the streets were full of food rubbish that nobody knew what to do with. Mountains of it sat in alleys. The international companies were not contracted to remove it. The Zabbaleen, without pigs, had no efficient way to process it. The government quietly admitted, in a 2010 report, that 'aspects of the previous arrangement were more functional than initially understood.'

In 2013 the city signed new contracts. The Zabbaleen were no longer trespassers. They became sub-contractors. The international companies still collected; the Zabbaleen still sorted. The split is not perfect, and the workshops are still mostly informal, but the recycling rate has risen again. It is not as high as before the pigs were killed.

The lesson many urban policy researchers now draw from Manshiyat Naser is not that informal systems are romantic. They are not. The Zabbaleen live with serious health problems, child labour, and very low incomes. The lesson is that any improvement that ignores what is already working will probably do worse than the thing it is replacing. The first questions a city must ask before signing a clean, modern contract are quiet ones: who is already doing this, what are they getting paid for, and what happens to them on the day the new trucks arrive?

annotations.meaningVi: đấu thầu công của thành phố
xâm phạm (đất / khu vực không phép)

grammarNotes.title: Passive voice + present perfect for accumulated processes
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng bị động và present perfect khi muốn nói quá trình đã tích tụ tới hiện tại. Trong bài: 'families in this community have collected household waste from across the city' (present perfect — kéo dài tới nay). 'The companies were obliged to recycle twenty per cent.' (bị động + nghĩa vụ). 'The Zabbaleen... found themselves trespassing on routes they had walked all their lives.' (past simple + past perfect). 'The recycling rate has risen again.' (present perfect — kết quả tới hiện tại).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the passive and present perfect for processes that have accumulated to the present. In the text: 'families in this community have collected household waste from across the city' (present perfect — sustained to now). 'The companies were obliged to recycle twenty per cent.' (passive + obligation). 'The Zabbaleen... found themselves trespassing on routes they had walked all their lives.' (past simple + past perfect). 'The recycling rate has risen again.' (present perfect — result reaching now).

translationVi:
Tại một khu phía đông nam Cairo gọi là Manshiyat Naser, khoảng bảy mươi nghìn người sống và làm việc dưới tên Zabbaleen. Từ này trong tiếng Ả Rập Ai Cập có nghĩa 'người làm rác'. Hơn một thế kỷ qua, các gia đình ở cộng đồng này thu gom rác sinh hoạt khắp thành phố. Họ phân loại bằng tay tại nhà. Họ nuôi lợn, lợn ăn phần hữu cơ. Họ bán giấy, thuỷ tinh, nhựa, kim loại cho các xưởng nhỏ, nhiều xưởng ngay trên những con phố phân loại. Theo ước tính độc lập, họ tái chế khoảng tám mươi phần trăm rác họ thu.

Năm 2003, thành phố ký hợp đồng với ba công ty xử lý rác quốc tế lớn. Hợp đồng hiện đại; thiết bị hiện đại; xe mới. Các công ty hứa, bằng ngôn ngữ đấu thầu công, một 'giải pháp sạch, hiệu quả'. Theo hợp đồng, các công ty bắt buộc phải tái chế hai mươi phần trăm.

Hợp đồng ban đầu không bao gồm Zabbaleen. Các công ty được giao khu độc quyền. Trong các khu đó, theo luật, chỉ công ty được ký mới được thu rác. Zabbaleen, hoạt động ở chính những phố đó nhiều thế hệ, bỗng thấy mình 'xâm phạm' những tuyến đường họ đã đi cả đời.

Sau năm năm kết quả đo được. Các công ty quốc tế trung bình tái chế từ mười một đến mười chín phần trăm. Zabbaleen, làm phi chính thức trong những khu họ còn vào được, vẫn tái chế khoảng tám mươi phần trăm. Tổng cộng Cairo tái chế ít rác hơn so với trước khi có hợp đồng hiện đại.

Lý do không bí ẩn. Công ty quốc tế bị đo theo việc thu — thùng có được dọn đúng giờ không. Họ được trả như nhau dù có tái chế hay không. Tái chế là chi phí; chôn lấp rẻ hơn. Ngược lại, Zabbaleen không được trả tiền thu. Họ được trả cho cái họ thu hồi. Mỗi kg nhựa là thu nhập. Động cơ của họ hướng tới tái chế vì lương họ hướng tới đó.

Năm 2009 một cuộc khủng hoảng riêng — hoảng loạn vì cúm lợn — khiến chính phủ Ai Cập ra lệnh giết toàn bộ lợn của Zabbaleen. Lợn đã ăn rác hữu cơ của Cairo cả thế kỷ. Trong vài tháng, đường phố đầy rác thực phẩm không ai biết xử lý sao. Núi rác chất trong ngõ. Công ty quốc tế không được ký để dọn. Zabbaleen không lợn không có cách xử lý hiệu quả. Chính phủ lặng lẽ thừa nhận trong báo cáo 2010 rằng 'các khía cạnh của thoả thuận trước có chức năng hơn so với ban đầu đánh giá'.

Năm 2013 thành phố ký hợp đồng mới. Zabbaleen không còn là kẻ xâm phạm. Họ thành nhà thầu phụ. Công ty quốc tế vẫn thu; Zabbaleen vẫn phân loại. Phân chia chưa hoàn hảo, các xưởng phần lớn vẫn phi chính thức, nhưng tỉ lệ tái chế đã tăng lại. Chưa cao bằng trước khi giết lợn.

Bài học các nhà nghiên cứu chính sách đô thị rút ra từ Manshiyat Naser không phải hệ thống phi chính thức là lãng mạn. Không. Zabbaleen sống với nhiều vấn đề sức khoẻ, lao động trẻ em, thu nhập rất thấp. Bài học là: bất kỳ cải tiến nào bỏ qua thứ đang chạy có lẽ sẽ làm tệ hơn cái nó thay thế. Câu hỏi đầu một thành phố phải hỏi trước khi ký hợp đồng sạch, hiện đại là những câu lặng: ai đang làm việc này, họ được trả cho gì, và họ sẽ ra sao vào ngày xe tải mới tới?

questions.prompt: Who are the Zabbaleen and what do they do?
options: ~70,000 people in Manshiyat Naser who have collected household waste for over a century; sort it at home; pigs eat the organic part; recycle ~80%
City signed contracts with three large international waste companies; promised 'clean, efficient solution'; contract recycling target 20%

questions.prompt: What happened to the Zabbaleen under the new contracts?
options: Excluded from exclusive zones; effectively trespassing on routes they had walked all their lives
International companies: 11–19% recycled; Zabbaleen ~80% in zones they could still reach; Cairo recycled less in total than before

questions.prompt: Why did the companies recycle less?
options: Paid the same whether they recycled or not; recycling cost > landfill; their incentive was collection on time

questions.prompt: Why did the Zabbaleen recycle more?
options: Paid per kilogram recovered — their wages ran in the recycling direction
Swine-flu panic; government ordered slaughter of the Zabbaleen's pigs; food rubbish piled in alleys

questions.prompt: What did the government admit in 2010?
options: 'Aspects of the previous arrangement were more functional than initially understood'
New contracts; Zabbaleen became sub-contractors; companies collect, Zabbaleen sort; rate rising but not at pre-pig levels

questions.prompt: What is the lesson researchers draw?
options: Improvement that ignores what is already working tends to do worse — ask who is already doing it, what they're paid for, and what happens to them

questions.prompt: Which is the article NOT claiming?
options: That informal systems are always better and should be left untouched
That Zabbaleen face health and child-labour problems
That ignoring existing systems can make things worse
questions.explanation: The text explicitly refuses to romanticise.
questions.hint: Look at the 'not that informal systems are romantic' line.

options: A modern, formal solution that ignores the incentives and capacity of an existing informal system can be less effective than what it replaces — and bringing the informal system in as a partner can recover most of what was lost.
Cairo should hire only foreign firms.

cloze.template:
In a neighbourhood on the south-eastern edge of Cairo called Manshiyat Naser, about seventy thousand people live and work as the Zabbaleen. The word means, in Egyptian Arabic, 'the {{b1}} people'. For more than a century, families in this community have collected household waste from across the city. They sort it by hand at home. They keep pigs, which eat the {{b2}} part. They sell the paper, glass, plastic and metal to small workshops, many of them on the same streets where the sorting happens. By independent estimates, they recycle about eighty per cent of what they collect.

In 2003, the city signed contracts with three large international waste companies. The contracts were modern; the equipment was modern; the trucks were new. The contracts did not, at first, include the Zabbaleen. The companies were given {{b4}} zones. In those zones, by law, only the contracted company could collect waste. The Zabbaleen, working in those same streets for generations, found themselves trespassing on routes they had walked all their lives.

The results, after five years, were measurable. The international companies were, on average, recycling between eleven and nineteen per cent of what they collected. The reason is not mysterious. The international companies were measured on collection — on whether the bins were emptied on time. They were paid the same whether they recycled or not. Recycling was a cost; landfill was {{b6}}. The Zabbaleen, by contrast, did not get paid for collection. They got paid for what they recovered. Every kilogram of plastic was their income. Their {{b7}} ran in the direction of recycling because their wages did.

In 2009 a separate crisis — a panic about {{b8}} flu — led the Egyptian government to order the slaughter of the Zabbaleen's pigs. The pigs had been eating Cairo's organic waste for a century. criticalThinkingQuestion: The researchers say the first questions are 'who is already doing this, what are they getting paid for, and what happens to them on the day the new trucks arrive?' Pick a 'modern improvement' in your own town — a new app, a new shop, a new road. Ask those three questions about it. What does the answer change?
